gist: Luke Groman argues Bitcoin is the 'last functioning smoke alarm of liquidity' and is being drained by AI, oil and commodities sucking liquidity out of the system. His intermediate/long-term base case: stocks up in dollars but down in gold and Bitcoin terms, much higher gold and Bitcoin, crushed long bonds, with the Iran/Hormuz crisis and US 'Suez moment' driving secular inflation, a weaker dollar, and a global shift to gold-settled, no-trust ('no ticky, no washi') trade.

insights.claim: Luke Groman has sold most of his Bitcoin and as of this recording has not bought back in any real way, only nibbling a little, because Bitcoin's weak price action (vs all-time-high stocks) is signaling deteriorating liquidity.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: A prominent macro analyst is positioned out of BTC and waiting for lower prices; his re-entry would be a liquidity all-clear signal.
insights.quote: I didn't sell it all but I sold most of it. Um so the answer is no I haven't.

insights.claim: Groman frames Bitcoin as 'one of, if not the last functioning smoke alarm of liquidity,' so its recent weakness is read as a warning that system-wide liquidity is being drained, not as a Bitcoin-specific problem.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Treat BTC price action as a real-time liquidity gauge; weakness while equities make highs is a system-wide tell, not idiosyncratic.
insights.quote: Bitcoin's one of, if not the last functioning smoke alarm of liquidity, and it's telling us not good things.

insights.claim: Groman cites Northstar & Bad Charts (analysts he likes on technicals) projecting a Bitcoin cycle bottom in Q3/Q4 somewhere in the $40Ks, and concedes 'I might get my number' rather than a $60K bottom, indicating he sees meaningful further downside as plausible.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: A $40K-range bottom in late 2026 would be a far better entry than current levels; sets a watch zone for accumulation.
insights.quote: they're predicting like Q3 Q4 bottom somewhere in the 40ks. Like I really think that we could see that. I might get my number.

insights.claim: Bitcoin was trading around $68,000 at the time of recording per Groman's offhand reference, a level he uses as a baseline against the ~$168K he'd expect if Iran were settling oil in size via Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Anchors the episode's price context (~$68K) and implies large sovereign BTC demand is NOT currently present.

themes.theme: AI sucking liquidity out of everything

insights.claim: Groman's working hypothesis is that AI, oil, and commodities are each draining liquidity out of the system, so everything not tied to those three (including Bitcoin) is flat to down despite headline indices at all-time highs.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Explains the BTC/equity disconnect; favors owning the liquidity magnets (AI, energy, commodities) over the drained 'everything else' until liquidity broadens.
insights.quote: AI is sucking all the oxygen out of the room, all the liquidity out of the room, and it's all in one area. And I think that's happening to Bitcoin as well.

insights.claim: Market breadth is very poor: per a chart Groman cites, ex-AI stocks the S&P 500 is flat to down slightly versus the day before the Iran war started, meaning the index gains are concentrated in roughly seven stocks.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Headline index strength masks a weak underlying market; a stumble in the AI complex could expose the index with little cushion.
insights.quote: X things related to AI, the S&P 500 is flat to down slightly uh from the day before the Iran war started.

insights.claim: Emerging-market outperformance is an illusion driven by a few AI/memory names: stripping out TSMC, Samsung, and another large AI/memory name, MSCI EM is actually getting crushed relative to MSCI US.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Don't mistake EM index strength for a broad EM bull case; the move is a narrow AI-supply-chain bet.
insights.quote: if you take those three or four AI related names out, AI's or EM is actually getting crushed.

insights.claim: AI buildout accounting front-loads reported earnings: revenues are booked up front while capex is amortized over a longer period, so the faster and more cash-flow-negative the buildout, the higher and faster reported earnings rise even as companies become cash-poor and shift from cash funding to borrowing more.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: High reported AI earnings can mask deteriorating cash flow and rising debt; watch the cash-flow statement and incremental borrowing, not just EPS.
insights.quote: the faster you build and the more cash flow negative you are the higher and faster your reported earnings will go

insights.claim: When the AI buildout slows for any reason (commodity shortages, chip holdups, data-center permitting delays), the accounting flips: earnings slow sharply or decline but become largely non-cash while cash flow actually rises meaningfully.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: A buildout slowdown is the key inflection; the earnings-down/cash-up signature is the tell to watch for the regime change in AI names.
insights.quote: the second it slows, essentially your revenue growth starts to slow and the lagged effect of the uh amortization... starts to catch up and then it flips.

insights.claim: Groman expects AI stocks to underperform once their buildout-driven earnings slow, because capital that chased the momentum leaves the space for whatever else had its oxygen sucked out, even though the stocks screen relatively cheap on valuation.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Cheap-looking AI valuations may not protect against a momentum-unwind rotation; the trigger is the earnings-growth slowdown.
insights.quote: if the earnings slow why do I own this versus... anything else that has had the oxygen sucked out of and so does capital start to leave the space

insights.claim: Unlike the 1999 dot-com bubble that burst under its own weight in free markets, today's AI boom is unlikely to be left to deflate because AI has been identified as a key battlefield of great-power competition, so the US government will likely support keeping the buildout going.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Government backstop means an AI top may be later and more managed than a 2000-style crash; betting on a clean self-inflicted bust may be premature.
insights.quote: AI has been identified as a key... battlefield of the great power competition and so... the government is likely going to support this

insights.claim: For AI valuations (as a percent of total US equity market cap) to be justified, AI must eviscerate white-collar employment; if instead big-tech leaders are right that AI won't disrupt employment, then it isn't the biggest thing since the internet and doesn't deserve those valuations.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Either outcome is bearish: no job destruction breaks the valuation/private-credit case; mass job destruction breaks US tax receipts. Frames AI as a fiscal/credit risk either way.
insights.quote: for the business case of AI to make any sense relative to the valuation... they have to kill employment. They have to kill white collar employment. That's the only way the math works.

insights.claim: Groman flags US labor-market weakness as evidence AI may already be biting: a recent month showed only 95,000 jobs in a country of 350 million, versus the 200,000-300,000 'good month' of 20-30 years ago when the population was ~20% smaller.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Weak job growth pressures tax receipts (employment is ~half of US tax receipts) and could force political intervention in AI economics.
insights.quote: We had 95,000 jobs... in a month in a country of 350 million people. When I grew up, a good month was 200 300,000 jobs in a month.

insights.claim: Groman's intermediate-to-long-term base case is that this resolves with much higher gold and much higher Bitcoin, higher equities in dollar terms but lower equities in gold and Bitcoin terms, and a stable bond market with 10-year yields ranging roughly 3.75%-4.5%.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Core actionable framework: measure equity performance in gold/BTC terms, not dollars; own gold and BTC as the unit of account that wins.
insights.quote: the way this ends is with stocks up big in dollars and stocks down in gold and stocks down in Bitcoin.

insights.claim: Even with gold's selloff and an equity bounce, the average US stock is probably still down ~40% in gold terms since the Fed started hiking in 2022 (headline indices down ~20-30% in gold terms), illustrating the regime that has quietly been in place since 2022.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Dollar-denominated equity highs hide a major loss of purchasing power; gold has been the better store of value through the hiking cycle.
insights.quote: the average stock certainly is probably still down 40% in gold terms over the last 5 years.

insights.claim: Groman suggests authorities could suppress gold and Bitcoin in the short run via expansion of derivatives (the way gold has historically been managed), keeping Bitcoin in a '58 to 72K' range, though he believes this cannot work for Bitcoin in the long run.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Derivative-satisfied demand (e.g., buying calls instead of spot BTC) can cap price short-term; long-term Groman expects suppression to fail, favoring spot accumulation.
insights.quote: to the extent that you can expand derivatives... Somebody wants to own Bitcoin, but they're not buying Bitcoin. They're buying a call on Bitcoin.

insights.claim: The US reshoring and trade-rebalancing agenda mathematically requires a much weaker dollar against the Chinese yuan (and possibly yen and euro), which is structurally bullish for gold and Bitcoin even if politically resisted.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Trade policy is a structural tailwind for gold/BTC via dollar debasement; positions against the dollar align with stated US objectives.
insights.quote: all of those things cannot happen unless the dollar is much weaker uh against the Chinese yuan

insights.claim: Some US elements resist a weaker dollar and rising gold/BTC because those moves signal to the world 'you're just inflating,' creating Treasury-market financing problems, especially given the 50-60bp rise in the 10-year yield since the Iran war started.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Explains the policy tension behind optics management of gold/BTC; rising yields constrain how much inflation signaling officials will tolerate.
insights.quote: those things will be... communicating to the world, hey, you're just inflating. Uh and that creates some issues on the financing side with the Treasury market

insights.claim: US long-term bond futures are down ~90% against gold over the last 10 years while GDP rose over that period, which Groman argues shows 90% wasn't enough and bonds remain structurally vulnerable versus hard assets.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Long-duration Treasuries have been a wealth-destroyer in gold terms; Groman sees more downside ahead relative to gold/BTC.
insights.quote: long-term bond futures in the United States are down 90% 90% against gold over the last 10 years.

insights.claim: Rare earths are a tiny market by monetary cap and tonnage but underpin tens of trillions of US tech equity market cap, an 'inverted pyramid' where enormous valuations rest on a thin sliver of materials China largely controls.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: A small, China-controlled input is a systemic chokepoint for huge swaths of tech equity value; a supply squeeze has outsized downstream impact.
insights.quote: you've got tens of trillions of market cap in US equities... resting on this little sliver of of of in volume terms of rare earth.

insights.claim: China dominates rare-earth refining not just via reserves but through 30 years of work, more engineers, fewer environmental regulations, and likely under-discussed innovation in the actual refining machinery and processes, which the US would struggle to replicate.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Closing the gap requires mine, refine, engineering and educational base plus machines that may come from China and that China may refuse to sell, making 'reshoring' rare earths slow and expensive.
insights.quote: no one ever talks about whether they've innovated in the machinery the actual refining machinery and the the refining processes and my understanding is they have.

insights.claim: Groman notes the Trump administration's recent rule-breaking pattern of taking equity stakes in companies (citing Intel and 'MP') that have done well, suggesting government-backed US rare-earth names could benefit from the domestic buildout despite the old rule that government involvement makes for poor stocks.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: US rare-earth companies with government backing are a thematic long on supply-chain reshoring; Groman points investors to 'rare earth US companies' without naming tickers.
insights.quote: if you... Google rare earth US companies and you start sort of sniffing around those things, th those would be the types of companies that should stand to benefit

themes.theme: Iran, Hormuz closure & the US 'Suez moment'

insights.claim: Groman correctly predicted (in early-mid March) the Strait of Hormuz would stay closed through July 4th, with major brokers now saying it could remain closed through Labor Day, while admitting he got the market reaction 'dead wrong' so far.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: A prolonged Hormuz closure that markets have not priced is the central unpriced macro risk; complacency creates asymmetric downside.
insights.quote: I said there's going to be I would start preparing for it to be closed still on July 4th and people thought it was crazy

insights.claim: WTI crude was around $95 at recording and oil is up ~50% since the Iran war started, yet Groman argues that a known multi-month Hormuz closure would normally imply far higher oil, much lower equities, and higher yields, so the market has not yet priced the disruption.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: If pricing eventually catches up to a sustained closure, expect a sharp oil spike and equity/bond repricing; energy is the asymmetric long.
insights.quote: that would be wow. Like that would be oil way higher than $95 or wherever we are today on on WTI and markets would be way lower.

insights.claim: Groman likens the current calm to a man falling past the 40th floor saying 'this feels just like flying'; the 'sudden stop' that hurts comes when global oil inventories hit tank bottoms, which Exxon and Chevron have warned could come within two to three weeks.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: The tank-bottom moment is the catalyst that forces demand rationing via price; near-term (2-3 week) window flagged by majors makes energy the key trade to monitor.
insights.quote: It's not the... fall that kills you. It's a sudden stop. And the sudden stop in this case is when you start hitting tank bottoms.

insights.claim: The Hormuz standoff is a 'pain contest': Iran is sustained by morphine-hit supplies (Russia via the Caspian, China via rail) while the oil-short world is buffered by SPR and global inventory draws, and whoever hits their limit first loses.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Track SPR and global inventory levels versus Iran's backdoor supply; inventory exhaustion in the West is the trigger for a stagflationary oil shock.
insights.quote: It's a pain contest. It's unquestionably a pain contest.

insights.claim: Groman argues Iran retains more fire control over the Gulf than the US admits, evidenced by leaked reports that US Middle East bases were heavily hit, air defenses underperformed, and aircraft losses exceeded official figures, which is why insurers won't write coverage and the strait stays closed.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: If Iranian Gulf control is real and durable, the closure is structural not transient; the unpriced risk persists longer than markets assume.
insights.quote: the Iranians have more fire control of the Gulf than people want to admit.

insights.claim: Groman calls this a US 'Suez moment'; when Britain had its 1956 Suez moment, median UK inflation ran almost 7% per year for the next 20 years, implying a comparable US loss of status could be structurally inflationary.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: A US Suez analog implies a multi-decade inflationary regime, reinforcing the long-gold/long-Bitcoin, short-long-bond thesis.
insights.quote: When the Brits had their Suez moment in 56, the... median rate of inflation over the next 20 years was almost 7% per year for 20 years.

insights.claim: Groman expects the US loss in Iran/the Gulf to accelerate multicurrency energy pricing away from the dollar, meaning less global need to hold dollar reserves, more demand to hold gold, and ultimately the Fed (or banking-system proxies) printing money to absorb Treasuries because yields above ~4.6-4.8% on the 10-year are unaffordable.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Sets a hard yield ceiling (~4.6-4.8%) that forces eventual QE/monetization, a secularly inflationary outcome bullish for gold and BTC.
insights.quote: We can't really afford above 4.8 4.6 4.8% on the 10-year. So... at some point that somebody is going to be the Fed with printed money

insights.claim: Groman doubts reports that Iran is settling oil payments in Bitcoin at scale, reasoning that if it were, BTC would be near $168,000 rather than ~$68,000; he sees the bigger real signal in China's CIPS interbank payment volumes, which have exploded higher since March, implying yuan settlement that de facto means gold.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Watch CIPS volumes, not Bitcoin headlines, for evidence of de-dollarized energy settlement; the flow is going through yuan into gold.
insights.quote: I don't think Bitcoin would be at 68,000... it would be probably 168,000.

themes.theme: Gold settlement & the no-trust monetary order

insights.claim: For five of the last six months, non-monetary gold has been the single largest US export, bigger than aircraft and pharmaceutical preparations, with flows running from the US to Switzerland/London and onward to China and Hong Kong, de facto settling the US-China trade deficit in gold.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Gold is already functioning as trade-settlement money between the US and China; the largest marginal driver of the shrinking trade deficit is gold exports.
insights.quote: five of the last six months non-monetary gold has been the single largest export of the United States... bigger than aircraft, bigger than pharmaceutical preparations.

insights.claim: Groman argues gold trade-settlement 'needs to happen at a much higher gold price, otherwise we're going to run out of gold,' implying the structural flow itself is a mechanism for a higher gold price over time.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Physical gold outflows create a built-in upward repricing pressure; supports a structurally higher gold price target.
insights.quote: It needs to happen at a much higher gold price because otherwise we're going to run out of gold.

insights.claim: Groman speculates Bessent may have struck a deal where ~$4,500 gold buys ~$6,000 of Chinese rare earths, a good trade for the US that nonetheless implies gold prices move structurally higher over time.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: If gold is being bartered for strategic materials at favorable ratios, it cements gold's monetary role and a rising price path.
insights.quote: If... the world will sell him gold at 4,500 and he has struck some sort of deal with the Chinese that 4500 in gold will buy $6,000 of rare earths, that's a good deal.

insights.claim: Because IMF rules require reporting non-monetary gold but not monetary gold, central banks (or the US) can ship gold and reclassify it so it never appears in statistics, meaning the true sovereign gold flows to China may be larger and unrecorded.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Official gold-flow data understates the real shift; positioning should assume more gold is moving east than headlines show.
insights.quote: if you buy non-monetary gold and then you reclassify it as monetary it never has to show up.

insights.claim: Groman argues the world is moving to a 'no ticky, no washi' (no trust) settlement system where paper is accepted in payment but final settlement is in gold or commodities, evidenced by China placing offshore yuan clearing banks in every major gold hub (London, Switzerland, Dubai, Singapore, Hong Kong, Shanghai).
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: A gold-anchored, trust-minimized settlement architecture is being built now; gold (and potentially BTC) is the structural beneficiary of declining counterparty trust.
insights.quote: I will take your paper in payment, but in settlement, I am going to exchange your paper for something that's going to hold value.

insights.claim: Groman cites China's biggest courier opening a 2,000-ton gold vault at Hong Kong airport as physical proof-of-work infrastructure for gold settlement, asking why a courier would need a vault unless China is building a physical-gold (not paper-gold) clearing system.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Concrete buildout of physical gold logistics signals institutional commitment to gold-based settlement, a long-term structural bid for gold.
insights.quote: China's biggest courier... is opening a 2,000 ton gold vault at the Hong Kong airport.

insights.claim: Groman believes 70-something-year-old heads of state (Putin, Xi, Polish leadership) will trust physical gold next to their tanks over Bitcoin due to concerns about exchange back doors, so gold wins the sovereign settlement role for now even though he views Bitcoin as technologically superior digital gold.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Sovereign demand favors gold near-term; Bitcoin's sovereign settlement role is a longer-dated, not-yet-arrived thesis.
insights.quote: Are they going to go send me the gold and I'm going to put it right here next to all my tanks, all my missiles... They're making the latter choice.

insights.claim: Of 58 countries in 150 years that hit 130% debt-to-GDP, 57 defaulted, mostly via significant inflation; the lone exception was Japan, which is now itself inflating with a shrinking bond market, rising rates, and a stock market down ~20% in gold terms over five years.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Historical base rate (now effectively 58/58) argues the US debt path resolves through inflation; favors hard assets over long-duration bonds.
insights.quote: There have been 58 countries in 150 years of history that have had debt to GDP hit 130%. And as of three years ago, 57 of them defaulted on that debt mostly via... significant inflation.

insights.claim: The US crossed 130% debt-to-GDP in summer 2020; since then long-term US Treasuries have fallen ~50-60% against gold, which Groman frames as the inflationary resolution already underway ('it's not doom, it's just math').
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: The bond-vs-gold debasement has already begun and Groman expects it to continue; reinforces underweighting long bonds versus gold/BTC.
insights.quote: long-term US Treasury bonds have fallen, I think, 60% against gold, 50% against gold

insights.claim: US entitlements plus interest are already at or near 100% of federal revenues, so a stagflationary recession (rationing oil demand via price) would simultaneously drop revenues and raise interest expense, making a Hormuz-driven oil shock fiscally catastrophic for the West.
insights.speaker: Luke Groman
insights.so_what: Ties the oil/Hormuz risk directly to a fiscal doom-loop; an oil-driven recession forces monetization, the bullish-gold/bearish-bond endgame.
insights.quote: in the US at least... you're already at 100% of revenues or close to it in terms of your entitlements plus interest.
